title: Whitney Wolfe Herd, keynote address at '25 Judge ...
text:
Hello. Congratulations graduates. This
is very exciting. And to everybody here
cheering these graduates on. The energy
we feel up here is pure pride. And
that's because you all deserve it. So, I
just want to say congratulations to this
amazing class of 2025, but to each and
every one of you that made this possible
for them because there is no way I can
speak from experience that we could have
walked across the stage and done what
they've just done without the support of
everyone in this room. So, graduates, I
had prepared this long speech for you,
but I'm I'm going to wing it. I'm going
to pull an audible. And I think what I
am going to do is tell you what I wish I
knew when I was in your shoes. 18 don't
age me years ago. And this was inspired
by something that happened backstage. My
sister and I were back there with my
5-year-old and I said to her, "Wow, do
you remember when we were here and I was
complaining about how old we were
getting?" "Yes, it's alarming. You are
so young. You are at the beginning of
your journey. And I know it feels like
the end of an amazing chapter, which it
is, but you are just getting started.
This is day one of the rest of your
lives. And you can do anything because
you survived judge. And it was hard.
Take it from me. It's harder than it
looks. So I think the advice I'm going
to give you today, it really boils down
to one word. And I'm going to take this
in three parts. One word that I want to
impart with you today is to remember to
stay connected. And that is not on the
phones. Put those things away. Not right
now, but in general, it's too much. The
first part of connection is a connection
with yourselves. We've talked a lot and
heard from amazing people about the
importance of connecting with each other
and connecting with the world, your
community, giving back, doing good. But
the one thing I don't think I ever
learned, not in high school, college, or
along the way, and I wish I had, was to
have a deep connection with yourself.
Because that is really the longest
relationship you will have. And it gets
tough out there. It gets fun. It gets
magical, but it gets scary. And if you
can learn how to befriend yourself right
now, right here, and start to really
make this such an important part of your
lives, I promise you, you will never
feel alone. You will not feel lonely as
you go out into the world. So remember
to connect with yourself. And there's a
magical secret language here. It's
called your
intuition. I did not receive a diploma
because I was special. I actually am in
kind of shock and awe because so many of
you are technically more experienced
than I am. You could probably ask
several of these teachers here
today. I am here because I listened to
my
intuition. I trusted myself and I knew
the difference between what was right
for me and what was right for somebody
else. And I'm sorry I'm addressing the
students, but I think it's important
they hear this. Listen to your
intuition. The amount of times I was
told to go right or go left or up or
down by society by I love my family.
They're here today. But even my family
tried to point me in different
directions and I stayed true to myself.
So listen to yourself and trust
yourself. The second part of
connection and I think Judge has done a
great job of both instilling this in
myself and all of you is a connection to
a higher
power. Life gets
confusing. The unimaginable
unimaginable happens in both good and
bad. And the only thing that really can
carry you through is a belief in
something higher and bigger than
ourselves. So I don't care if you call
that God or spirit or universe, whatever
you identify as religiously or
spiritually, lean on that. It will carry
you through because life does get hard.
And the third part of
connection is a connection to each
other. So all of you are about to go all
over the country and maybe some of you
are staying here and maybe some of you
are moving to different parts of the
world. Don't lose sight of each other. I
called one of my best friends from judge
this morning, Abby Price, and I told
her, "What am I supposed to say?" And
she said, "Just get up there and just
talk about how we got our uniforms taken
away cuz our skirts were too short."
Said, "I cannot say that, Abby." So, you
know, these friendships will carry you
through the rest of your lives. Do not
lose sight of them. And it's going to be
amazing to see what you all do. One of
my best friends from Judge, he told me
freshman year in the library that he was
going to be a plastic surgeon. I told
him, "Great, I'll call you in 18 years.
I'm going to need a lot of help." He
said, "Great. I'll be a top plastic
surgeon." And guess what? He is. He
fulfilled that dream because he set the
intention. He believed in himself. He
had the foundation of Judge and his
higher education that he went on to
next. And he made it happen. Time will
pass. Chase your dreams. You have
nothing to lose. Now, there were
students like me that did not know what
they were going to do. They did not have
career paths. I was very much one of
those. But I will tell you what I did
have. I had a burning desire to do
something that mattered. Didn't know
what that was. No idea. But my high
school quote was from Walt Disney, the
person I admired most, because he built
this magical world that made people feel
something. He left people feeling
emotional when they engaged with the
world he created. And my quote was,
remember, if you can dream it, you can
do it. Because this all started with a
mouse and a dream. Now, all of you have
a dream. And a mouse is a pretty mundane
thing. So sometimes that's all we have
at your age is just a dream. So I'm
going to leave you with one piece of
homework and I'm going to stop speaking
very soon, I promise, and get let you
get on with your graduation festivities.
I know you don't want any more homework,
but this is important. I want you to set
your dreams right now. Go home before
you go out and celebrate. Uh parents,
we're going to need your help. This is a
bit analog for this crowd, but we need
an envelope and a piece of paper and a
pen. And I want you to write a letter to
your future selves. I want you to write
your names. And I want you to write
2043. I know that sounds scary, but that
is the distance that I am from where you
are today. And I want you to put
yourself in that present moment and tell
yourself where you are. What have you
achieved?
Where have you gone in the world? How
have you connected with yourself, with
others, with a higher power? And how
have you let that guide you to
fulfilling your greatest dreams? And
open it when you're my age, 35, and
maybe you'll be on this stage one day.
So with that, I just want you to
remember to stay connected. Real
connection is not on the phone. It's in
your hearts. It's in your spirit. It's
with each other. And if you can dream
it, you can absolutely do it because
you're here today. Congratulations,
class of 2025.
